vocabulary.title: come up with
vocabulary.source: 学習院大学25年法学部プラス方式

vocabulary.title: owe you one
vocabulary.source: 学習院大学25年法学部プラス方式

grammar.title: what
grammar.content: whatはそれ自体に先行詞を含んでいるため、直前に名詞（new wordsなど）がある場合は使えない。その場合は that や which を使う
grammar.source: 学習院大学25年法学部プラス方式

strategy.title: 逆接の後の筆者の主張
strategy.content: 長文の中で「But」や「However」が出た直後は、一般論を覆す筆者の重要な主張が来やすいので、そこを精読してマークする。
strategy.source: 学習院大学25年法学部プラス方式

meta.title: I don't have the slightest idea
meta.content: 意味・ルール: 過去問の会話文で "I don't have the slightest idea."（全くわからない）という表現が出たように、本番でも考え込んでも無駄な問題は執着せずにすぐ次へ行く。
meta.source: 学習院大学25年法学部プラス方式

vocab_books.title: 多義語対応-つよつよ候補

idioms.base_form: a rolling stone gathers no moss
all you have to do is
there is no need to do
when in Rome, do as the Romans do

chunks: ① Could I have **a piece of** that chocolate cake?
① My professor gave me **a piece of** valuable advice.

enriched_idioms.base_form: a rolling stone gathers no moss
meanings: ① 転石苔むさず（職業や住居を転々とする人は金がたまらない、または、活動的な人はいつまでも古びない）
chunks: ① My uncle moves to a new country every year, proving the old saying that **a rolling stone gathers no moss**.
① She is always trying new hobbies and staying active, as she believes that **a rolling stone gathers no moss**.
① The Japanese market **accounts for** 30% of our total sales.
① In this class, female students **account for** more than half the total.
② How do you **account for** the fact that you were absent yesterday?
② The severe weather **accounts for** the delay in the shipment.

meanings: ① ～に本格的に取り組む、～に専念する
chunks: ① The government must **address itself to** the problem of the aging population.
① After taking a short break, she began to **address herself to** her research.

meanings: ① ～に関するアドバイス、助言
chunks: ① I need some **advice on** which university I should apply to.
① He gave me some useful **advice on** how to prepare for the interview.

meanings: ① Aが～するのを許す、可能にする
chunks: ① My parents won't **allow me to stay** out late on weekdays.
① This new app **allows users to edit** photos easily.

enriched_idioms.base_form: all you have to do is
chunks: ① To turn on the computer, **all you have to do is** press this button.
① **All you have to do is** fill out this form to register for the event.

meanings: ① とりわけ、数ある中で
chunks: ① At the meeting, we discussed the schedule, the budget, and, **among other things**, our marketing strategy.
① He is interested in, **among other things**, classical music and modern art.
① She was **among those who** supported the new policy from the beginning.
① **Among those who** applied for the job, only a few had the necessary qualifications.
① **Apart from** being a great singer, she is also a talented actress.
① **Apart from** its historical significance, the city is famous for its beautiful parks.
② The office was empty **apart from** the security guard.
② I like all kinds of fruit **apart from** bananas.

meanings: ① ～から生じる、～に起因する
chunks: ① Many health problems **arise from** a poor diet and lack of exercise.
① The misunderstanding **arose from** a lack of communication.
① He studied hard for the exam. **As a result**, he got a perfect score.
① The train was delayed due to heavy snow. **As a result**, I was late for work.
① Some individual players are good, but the team is not strong **as a whole**.
① We need to consider the problem not in isolation, but **as a whole**.
① **As far as I am concerned**, this is the best solution to the problem.
① **As far as the new project is concerned**, everything is going according to plan.
① You can use my computer **as long as** you are careful with it.
① It doesn't matter what you do for a living, **as long as** you are happy.
② I will never forget your kindness **as long as** I live.
② You are welcome to stay here **as long as** you want.
① You can get a decent lunch for **as little as** 500 yen around here.
① The new device can be charged in **as little as** 15 minutes.
① He bought a new shirt, and he bought a tie **as well**.
① If you're ordering pizza, I'd like some **as well**.
① She is a talented musician **as well as** a skilled painter.
① This restaurant is known for its excellent food **as well as** its friendly service.
① He is not the kind of person I want my son to **associate with**.
① You can tell a lot about a person by the company they **associate with**.
② Most people **associate** the brand **with** high quality.
② I always **associate** the smell of cherry blossoms **with** the start of spring.
① The elevator can only carry ten people **at a time**.
① He was so hungry that he ate three hamburgers **at a time**.

meanings: ① よくても、せいぜい
chunks: ① His chances of passing the exam are, **at best**, 50 percent.
① The report was confusing and, **at best**, incomplete.
① There is a big party **at the end of** the school year.
① I'll get paid **at the end of** this month.
① I'm sorry, but I won't **be able to** attend the meeting tomorrow.
① After she recovered from her injury, she was **able to** run again.

meanings: ① ～のような体格をしている
chunks: ① That professional wrestler **is built like** a truck.
① He's an office worker, but he **is built like** a professional athlete.

meanings: ① ～に気をつける、注意する
chunks: ① You need to **be careful about** your spending if you want to save money.
① Please **be careful about** making noise late at night.
① The research team **is composed of** experts from various fields.
① The book **is composed of** ten chapters, each focusing on a different topic.
① The location of the hotel is very **convenient for** sightseeing.
① Would 3 p.m. on Friday **be convenient for** you?

meanings: ① ～のような形にデザインされている
chunks: ① The new museum **is designed like** a traditional Japanese castle.
① This bottle opener **is designed like** a key, making it easy to carry.
① This old bottle **is hard to open**.
① It **is hard to believe** that he is over 70 years old.
① The photocopier **is in use** at the moment.
① Is this seat **in use**, or can I take it?

idioms.base_form: the fact of the matter is

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜に由来する、〜から来る
chunks: ① The word "karaoke" **comes from** Japanese. (「カラオケ」という言葉は日本語に由来する。)
① Where do you **come from** originally? (もともとのご出身はどちらですか？)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 考え・解決策を思いつく、考案する
chunks: ① She **came up with** a brilliant idea for the new project. (彼女は新しいプロジェクトの素晴らしいアイデアを思いついた。)
① We need to **come up with** a solution to this problem quickly. (私たちはこの問題の解決策を早急に思いつく必要がある。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜を利用する、活用する
chunks: ① You should **take advantage of** this opportunity to travel abroad. (あなたはこの海外旅行の機会を活用すべきだ。)
① Let's **take advantage of** the good weather and go for a picnic. (天気が良いのを利用してピクニックに行こう。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 徐々に浸透する、普及する
chunks: ① The new technology began to **spread in** the market. (その新しい技術は市場で徐々に普及し始めた。)
① A feeling of excitement **spread in** the crowd. (興奮した感情が群衆の中に浸透していった。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜なので、〜であるため
chunks: ① We need to book our tickets early, **as there are** limited seats available. (座席数に限りがあるので、私たちは早めにチケットを予約する必要がある。)
① **As there are** many questions, let's extend the Q&A session. (多くの質問があるため、質疑応答の時間を延長しましょう。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜を超える、〜を越えていく
chunks: ① The movie's success **went beyond** everyone's expectations. (その映画の成功は皆の期待をはるかに超えた。)
① Her duties **go beyond** just answering the phone. (彼女の職務は単に電話に出ることを超えている。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜から二酸化炭素を除去する
chunks: ① This new filter can **eliminate carbon dioxide from** the air. (この新しいフィルターは空気から二酸化炭素を除去できる。)
① The goal is to **eliminate carbon dioxide from** industrial emissions. (目標は産業排出物から二酸化炭素を除去することだ。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜によって導入される、紹介される
chunks: ① A new law will **be introduced by** the government next year. (新しい法律が来年、政府によって導入される予定だ。)
① The keynote speaker **was introduced by** the event organizer. (基調講演者はイベントの主催者によって紹介された。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 不明瞭さ、あいまいさ
chunks: ① The **lack of clarity** in his explanation confused everyone. (彼の説明の不明瞭さが皆を混乱させた。)
① There is a **lack of clarity** about the project's main objectives. (そのプロジェクトの主目的についてあいまいさがある。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 意味がある、重要である
chunks: ① His promises don't **mean anything** to me anymore. (彼の約束はもう私にとって何の意味もない。)
① I don't know if this old map will **mean anything**, but let's take it. (この古い地図が重要かどうか分からないが、持っていこう。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜を偶然見つける、〜に偶然出くわす
chunks: ① I **came across** some old letters while cleaning out the attic. (屋根裏を掃除しているときに、古い手紙をいくつか偶然見つけた。)
① Yesterday, I **came across** a friend I hadn't seen in years. (昨日、何年も会っていなかった友人に偶然出くわした。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜に由来する、〜から来る
chunks: ① The town's name **turns off** an ancient legend. (その町の名前は古代の伝説に由来する。)
① His unique artistic style **turns off** his childhood experiences. (彼の独特な芸術スタイルは、彼の子ども時代の経験から来る。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜することを許可されている、〜してもよい
chunks: ① Visitors **are allowed to** take photos in this area. (来場者はこのエリアで写真を撮ることを許可されている。)
① You **are not allowed to** enter the room without permission. (許可なくその部屋に入ることは許されていない。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① （2つのものの中で）より軽い
chunks: ① Of the two suitcases, I will take **the lighter** one. (2つのスーツケースのうち、より軽い方を私が持っていきます。)
① Please hand me **the lighter** of the two bags. (2つのカバンのうち、より軽い方を私に渡してください。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 追加料金なしで、無料で
chunks: ① The hotel provides breakfast **at no extra charge**. (そのホテルは追加料金なしで朝食を提供している。)
① You can get a refill of your coffee **at no extra charge**. (コーヒーのおかわりを追加料金なしでいただけます。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① かなりの、相当な（時間・距離など）
chunks: ① The nearest town is **a good** ten miles from here. (最寄りの町はここからかなりの10マイル離れている。)
① We waited for **a good** hour before the bus arrived. (バスが到着するまで、私たちは相当な1時間待った。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜する余地、〜の可能性、〜の機会
chunks: ① There is always **room for** improvement. (常に改善の余地はある。)
① Is there any **room for** dessert? (デザートを食べる余裕はありますか？)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜に対するセンス、〜の好み
chunks: ① He has excellent **taste in** music. (彼は音楽に対するセンスが素晴らしい。)
① She and I have a similar **taste in** movies. (彼女と私は映画の好みが似ている。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 当分の間、今のところは
chunks: ① Let's keep the plan as it is **for the time being**. (当分の間、計画は現状のままにしておきましょう。)
① **For the time being**, this office will be our temporary workspace. (今のところは、このオフィスが私たちの一時的な仕事場になります。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 好きなように、自由に
chunks: ① He could change the settings of the machine **at will**. (彼はその機械の設定を好きなように変更できた。)
① The user can customize the layout **at will**. (ユーザーはレイアウトを自由にカスタマイズできる。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜に慣れている
chunks: ① He **is at home in** front of a large audience. (彼は大観衆の前でも慣れている。)
① After a few weeks, she began to **be at home in** her new school. (数週間後、彼女は新しい学校に慣れ始めた。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① （電話で）待つ、しばらく待つ
chunks: ① Could you **hold on** for a moment, please? (少々お待ちいただけますか？)
① **Hold on**, I'm coming as fast as I can. (ちょっと待って、できるだけ速く向かっているから。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜を重んじる、〜を高く評価する
chunks: ① He doesn't **make much of** his own accomplishments. (彼は自分自身の業績をあまり高く評価しない。)
① The school **makes much of** students' individual creativity. (その学校は生徒一人ひとりの創造性を重んじる。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜を除外する、〜の可能性を否定する
chunks: ① We cannot completely **rule out** the possibility of an error. (我々はエラーの可能性を完全に除外することはできない。)
① The police have **ruled out** suicide in this case. (警察はこの事件における自殺の可能性を否定した。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 徐々に浸透する、普及する
chunks: ① The new policy's benefits are expected to **trickle down** to the poor. (新政策の恩恵が貧困層に徐々に浸透することが期待されている。)
① Information from the headquarters finally began to **trickle down** to branch offices. (本社からの情報がようやく支社に浸透し始めた。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜を引き出す、吸い出す
chunks: ① A good teacher knows how to **draw out** the potential of each student. (良い教師は、各生徒の可能性を引き出す方法を知っている。)
① The dentist had to **draw out** the infected tooth. (歯医者は感染した歯を抜かなければならなかった。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜を利用する、活用する
chunks: ① This software can **be used by** people of all ages. (このソフトウェアはあらゆる年齢の人々によって利用されうる。)
① The library's resources should **be used by** students to the fullest. (図書館の資料は学生によって最大限に活用されるべきだ。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜に適用される、〜に塗布される
chunks: ① The new safety regulations will **be applied to** all factories. (新しい安全規則はすべての工場に適用される。)
① Sunscreen should **be applied to** the skin 30 minutes before going out. (日焼け止めは外出の30分前に肌に塗布されるべきだ。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① 〜によって損傷を受ける、悪影響を受ける
chunks: ① The building **was** badly **damaged by** the earthquake. (その建物は地震によってひどく損傷を受けた。)
① His car **was damaged by** falling branches during the storm. (彼の車は嵐の間に落ちてきた枝によって損傷を受けた。)

enriched_idioms.meanings: ① AからBへ移行する、変化する
chunks: ① The traffic light **went from a** red **to b** green. (信号が赤から青へ変わった。)
① In just a decade, the city has **gone from a** quiet town **to b** a bustling metropolis. (わずか10年で、その市は静かな町から賑やかな大都市へと変化した。)

exam_records.exam_name: 2026年 日本大学 文理\哲 全学統一N1
2025年 日本大学 文理\哲 全学統一N1

saved_review_session.name: 25年日本大学文理学部
saved_review_session.text:
以下に、ご指定のPDFファイルから抽出したテキストデータを、問題の構造がわかるように整形したものです。

***

# 2025-N1 英語 試験問題

## I

次の英文(1)~(6)の空所 [ 1 ] ~ [ 6 ] を埋めるのに最も適切なものを、それぞれ下の選択肢①~④から1つずつ選びなさい。

**(1)** If you [ 1 ] across anything suspicious, please report it to the station attendant.
① would come
② should come
③ were coming
④ had come

**(2)** Our family made a decision [ 2 ] a dog from the shelter.
① to adopt
② adopting
③ from adopting
④ with adopted

**(3)** The staff requested that everyone in the audience [ 3 ] off their cell phones during the concert.
① turn
② turned
③ would turn
④ have turned

**(4)** How many [ 4 ] are we allowed to bring on board on international flights?
① luggage
② luggages
③ piece of luggages
④ pieces of luggage

**(5)** I chose [ 5 ] of the two coats because it's more comfortable to wear.
① lightest
② lighter
③ the lighter
④ the lightest

**(6)** I can tell he is staying in Japan for the first time by [ 6 ] he behaves.
① the beginning
② the way
③ what
④ that

## II

次の英文(1)~(6)の空所 [ 7 ] ~ [ 12 ] を埋めるのに最も適切なものを、それぞれ下の選択肢①~④から1つずつ選びなさい。

**(1)** Litter [ 7 ] the ocean scenery, and it negatively affects sea life.
① spoils
② misses
③ suffers
④ loses

**(2)** As a member, you are welcome to use the club facilities at no extra [ 8 ].
① tax
② price
③ fare
④ charge

**(3)** It will take a [ 9 ] six hours to get to Tokyo by car.
① lot
② far
③ much
④ good

**(4)** The steak I had was so [ 10 ] that I couldn't chew it.
① tough
② hard
③ strong
④ tight

**(5)** We could see little [ 11 ] for negotiation in the meeting.
① space
② home
③ place
④ room

**(6)** My brother has good [ 12 ] in fashion, so I often rely on him to help me choose my outfits.
① mood
② style
③ taste
④ preference

## III

次の英文(1)~(6)の空所 [ 13 ] ~ [ 18 ] を埋めるのに最も適切なものを、それぞれ下の選択肢①~④から1つずつ選びなさい。

**(1)** Jenny is driving her mother's car [ 13 ] until she gets her own.
① at that time
② in the long run
③ for the time being
④ in no time

**(2)** Once you set the password on your account page, you can change it [ 14 ].
① in advance
② at will
③ by degree
④ in sight

**(3)** Even though Michael has never been to Japan, he is [ 15 ] the language and culture.
① in honor of
② at home in
③ in the light of
④ at the mercy of

**(4)** Can you [ 16 ] for a second? I need to find a pen to write down your address.
① hold on
② hang up
③ ring off
④ talk over

**(5)** Our boss [ 17 ] punctuality and expects us to be on time for meetings.
① gives rise to
② catches sight of
③ makes much of
④ keeps pace with

**(6)** The prime minister doesn't [ 18 ] the possibility of a tax increase within the next five years.
① result in
② come about
③ set up
④ rule out

## IV

次の英文を読み、空所 [ 19 ] ~ [ 23 ] を埋めるのに文脈上最も適切なものを、それぞれ下の選択肢①~④から1つずつ選びなさい。

Long ago, people lived as hunters and gatherers. Over time, people learned how to grow plants and raise domestic animals. Once this happened, there was a transition to agriculture in many societies. However, even today, there are groups who [ 19 ] farming and continue to hunt animals and gather their own food.

The Hadza people are a group of hunter-gatherers who live in an isolated part of northern Tanzania. They have lived in the Great Rift Valley for a period of 10,000 years. The Hadza communicate in their own language, called Hadzane.

The Hadza are not part of the modern economic system of Tanzania. When they are hungry, they can hunt or gather what they need [ 20 ]. The Hadza can make a little money by displaying their hunting skills for tourists. It is an interesting [ 21 ] that although the Hadza have very little, they share a lot. In fact, they share everything they have.

In the Great Rift Valley, modern farming has spread in recent years, and this development has had [ 22 ] consequences for the Hadza. Their homeland is now only 25 percent of the size it was in the 1950s. Hunting is now more difficult for them as there are fewer animals than before. The Hadza people anticipate that their way of life will [ 23 ] in the near future.

**[ 19 ]**
① reject
② promote
③ possess
④ conduct

**[ 20 ]**
① in vain
② for free
③ by nature
④ on duty

**[ 21 ]**
① contradiction
② addiction
③ discrimination
④ reputation

**[ 22 ]**
① ideal
② little
③ serious
④ migrant

**[ 23 ]**
① develop
② arise
③ expand
④ disappear

## V

次の英文を読み、[ 24 ] ~ [ 28 ] の各文を完成させるのに最も適切なものを、それぞれ下の選択肢①~④から1つずつ選びなさい。*の付いた語には [Notes]があります。

The fashion industry is being forced to evaluate its role in climate change. With evidence pointing towards an impending environmental crisis if carbon dioxide (CO₂) emissions are not curbed, clothing companies are striving to make their products more sustainable. Yet, even so, the amount of waste that comes from global textile production is staggering, with some studies estimating that the industry produces over 1.2 billion tons of carbon dioxide a year. That's more than the annual airline and maritime* shipping emissions combined.

Considering this predicament, a new term has been proposed to replace the word sustainability: "climate positivity." With sustainability, the aim is to lower the amount of carbon emissions in the manufacturing process, whereas, with climate positivity, the aim is to go beyond reducing the carbon footprint to actually create a benefit to the environment by eliminating carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. The term “climate positivity" was first introduced by the non-profit organization Slow Factory Foundation because of a perceived lack of clarity coming from many fashion companies about how sustainable their products are. According to the director of the Slow Factory Foundation, "'Sustainable fashion' is becoming a buzzword, but it doesn't mean anything. Brands are claiming sustainability left and right. And, unfortunately, it's getting trapped at the surface in the marketing department; it's not trickling down deep, deeper into the roots of the problem."

So, what kinds of processes could be considered climate positive? In fact, there are some very interesting developments being made in textile manufacturing that could revolutionize the way clothing is designed. For example, Charlotte McCurdy, a fashion designer from New York, recently designed a raincoat made from algae*. Algae can draw carbon out of the atmosphere, so this raincoat actually cleans the environment as it is worn. Algae is being used by other designers, as well. For instance, Post Carbon Lab, a start-up based in London, has developed a coating of algae that can be applied to fabric. Through photosynthesis*, this coating absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and emits oxygen. According to the company, one large T-shirt with this coating generates as much oxygen as a six-year-old oak tree. Eventually, it is speculated that this coating could be used on shoes, jackets, backpacks, curtains, and other products. Of course, algae-based clothing needs special care. The clothing is damaged by washing machines, so it needs to be handwashed. Also, algae need light and carbon dioxide to survive, so the clothing should be put in a sunny place.

Another advancement that could be considered climate positive is the development of leather substitutes. Examples are Pinatex, made from pineapple leaves, Mycotex, made from mushrooms, and Desserto, made from cactus. The leather industry puts a huge strain on the environment, so using plant products, instead, helps create a cleaner atmosphere.

With such innovations, the fashion industry has the potential to go from being part of the environmental problem to being part of the solution. While the clock is ticking in regard to how much time we have to avert climate change, solutions are being developed that give us reasons to be hopeful.

**[Notes]**
*   maritime 「海運の」
*   algae「藻類」
*   photosynthesis「光合成」

**24.** One problem the author addresses in the beginning is that
① the fashion industry is usually not blamed for climate change.
② some clothing companies produce more carbon dioxide than others.
③ textile production is responsible for a great deal of carbon dioxide emissions.
④ most clothing companies ship their products by air or sea.

**25.** The aim of climate positivity is to
① prevent climate change.
② remove carbon dioxide from the air in order to benefit the environment.
③ decrease the amount of carbon emissions.
④ promote sustainability in the manufacturing process.

**26.** The Slow Factory Foundation was
① aware that many fashion companies wanted to make their products more sustainable.
② asked to come up with a new term to promote sustainability in the fashion industry.
③ suspicious about the level of sustainability claimed by many fashion companies.
④ established by some fashion companies to promote their products under one brand.

**27.** One thing to be careful about regarding algae-based clothing is that
① algae can only be used for particular types of clothing such as raincoats.
② it takes time for algae to absorb carbon dioxide and emit oxygen.
③ it needs to be washed more often than other types of clothing.
④ light and carbon dioxide are required for algae to stay alive.

**28.** The author mentions leather substitutes to
① introduce a new way for the fashion industry to save animals and plants in the wild.
② give an example of one of the efforts to develop environmentally friendly products.
③ show how everyday foods can be turned into products that last a long time.
④ emphasize how much harm the fashion industry is causing to the environment.

## VI

次の会話文を読み、[ 29 ] ~ [ 33 ] の各文を完成させるのに最も適切なものを、それぞれ下の選択肢①~④から1つずつ選びなさい。*の付いた語句には [Notes] があります。

**May:** I can't believe that a pharmaceutical* company is going to fund our new science lab.
**Danny:** Oh, really, they are? That's really great news; we need a new lab.
**May:** I'm surprised to hear you say that. I think that it's terrible news.
**Danny:** Why's that? What's important is that we have a good, modern lab with facilities such as IT. The current one is a dinosaur.
**May:** That may be true, but do you want to look at a company logo every time you walk into the lab?
**Danny:** Frankly, I don't care. What I want to see is a well-equipped lab, whatever the logo.
**May:** Give me a break. The fact of the matter is that "gifts" like these don't come without strings attached. We know why soda companies sponsor school teams. They do it on the condition that they have the exclusive right to put vending machines in the schools. It increases their sales even though soda doesn't exactly lend itself to healthy living.
**Danny:** You have a point. But big corporations are not all bad. Some software companies are working with libraries to provide technology and training.
**May:** Don't get me wrong. I'm not saying they're all bad. But you have to wonder why a company would give all that money for a lab if there wasn't some payback for them. I wouldn't be surprised if a lot of the research we carry out will now be for their benefit. What if we found out things they don't agree with — would they be published?
**Danny:** Don't be so paranoid. They would have to allow us to be independent.
**May:** I hate to say this, but I think you're being naive.
**Danny:** Well, I might be naive, but at least I'll be working in a state-of-the-art lab.

**[Notes]**
*   pharmaceutical 「製薬の」

**29.** May tells Danny that
① she has been offered a job at a pharmaceutical company.
② their science lab will be financially supported by a company.
③ a new lab is under construction at the facility that they work at.
④ their science lab will focus on studying dinosaurs from now on.

**30.** May talks about soda companies sponsoring school teams to
① point out the benefits of working together with commercial companies.
② argue that many commercial companies only do things that benefit themselves.
③ claim that schools should be blamed for promoting an unhealthy lifestyle.
④ explain how vending machines were first introduced to schools.

**31.** Danny and May agree that
① some companies provide services and support to others without bad intentions.
② big corporations need to work with researchers in different industries.
③ software companies are beneficial to society as they provide technology.
④ using new software is the key to staying up to date with the latest technology.

**32.** Danny thinks that
① May has a point in saying their lab needs money.
② May is wrong about their lab being independent.
③ May will find things that will be for the benefit of others.
④ May is failing to see the bright side of the news.

**33.** In this conversation, Danny and May
① figure out a solution to the problem presented in the beginning.
② come up with different ways to get support for their lab.
③ express different views on the topic being discussed.
④ come to an agreement about what they should do in the current situation.

## VIII

次の [ 40 ] ~ [ 45 ] の英文を完成させるように、[ ]内の①~⑤を並べかえ,その5つの中で3番目にくる語句の番号を選びなさい。*の付いた語句には [Notes] があります。

Before modern science, people [ 40 ] cure all kinds of diseases but also to stay healthy and avoid becoming sick.
[ ① only ② used ③ not ④ to ⑤ plants ]
In the United States, native people used more than 3,000 different plants as medicine. Today, people throughout the world still use plants to improve their health. In fact, many of our modern medicines [ 41 ] plants.
[ ① that ② come ③ include ④ from ⑤ some things ]

Some of the most popular plants used for health are called herbs. Medicines made with herbs use whole plants or parts of plants, such as the flowers, seeds, roots, leaves, and fruits, to prevent or treat illnesses. Herbal medicines can be eaten in food, drunk as a tea, or applied directly to the skin. In many nations, governments do not recognize herbal medicines. Doctors cannot give them to sick persons. But still, most people in the world — perhaps as many as [ 42 ] — have tried herbal medicine at least once.
[ ① eight ② ten ③ out ④ every ⑤ of ]
There are special stores that sell herbal medicines. In addition, some kinds of herbs are sold in supermarkets and can be used for cooking.

Ginseng* is one of the most common herbal medicine plants. It is said to reduce blood pressure [ 43 ].
[ ① the body ② disease ③ and ④ fight ⑤ help ]
Another plant frequently used for its healthy benefits is garlic. Garlic, which is often used in cooking, helps prevent heart disease and colds. Another common cooking plant, ginger, helps cure upset stomachs. Some flowers are thought to be strong herbal medicines. One kind of flower (Echinacea*) may help people who have serious diseases, while another (St. John's wort*) has been shown to help people who are feeling [ 44 ].
[ ① trouble ② having ③ sleeping ④ or ⑤ sad ]

Scientists use many of the natural substances from plants to make different types of modern medicine. But true herbal medicine generally uses the whole plant, not just one part of the plant. Also, herbal medicine will mix several different herbs together in the same medicine. Another difference between herbal medicine and modern medicine is that herbal medicines are made to help a person's whole body — not just to cure a problem in one part of the body.

Today, more and more people are recognizing the value of herbal medicines, and some doctors are even starting to recommend them. But even for those who prefer taking only modern medicine, chances [ 45 ] that medicine comes from plants.
[ ① that ② least part ③ at ④ of ⑤ are ]

**[Notes]**
*   ginseng 「チョウセンニンジン」
*   Echinacea 「ムラサキバレンギク」
*   St. John's wort 「セイヨウオトギリソウ」
